title: Macramé mirror frame
subtitle: A knotted cord frame for a circular mirror with decorative fringe, approximately 40 cm diameter overall
excerpt: A macramé mirror frame is built by mounting cords onto a metal ring and knotting them outward around a circular mirror. The frame works on a small scale without complex pattern sequences; the main design element is a combination of lark's head mounts, half-hitch rows, and a lower fringe.
sourceNotes: Macramé mirror frame construction is documented in contemporary UK and US macramé instruction. The lark's head and half-hitch sequence described is consistent across sources. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A folded-cord mounting knot used to attach working cords to a ring or dowel. Fold each cord at its midpoint, lay the fold over the ring, and pull both tails through the loop from front to back. Lark's head knots grip securely and can be pushed together tightly around the full circumference of a ring.

A single loop made by passing a working cord around a carrier cord and pulling the end through. Used in rows to form diagonal or horizontal bands across a macramé piece. Two half-hitches in opposite directions make a square knot; consecutive half-hitches in the same direction from the same cord produce a spiral.

The trailing cord ends that hang below a macramé piece, formed by the working cord lengths that remain after knotting is complete. Fringe can be trimmed to a level edge, cut in a V or arc shape, or left at natural varied lengths. Unravelling the plied cord ends produces a brushed or feathered fringe effect.

content.text: A macramé mirror frame uses a metal ring as the structural element: the ring holds the mirror in place and provides the mounting point for all cords. Cords are attached with
packed tightly around the top half of the ring, then rows of
lock the cords at the sides. The lower cords end in a
that hangs below the mirror and gives the piece its decorative weight.

items.name: Metal ring, 25 cm inner diameter, to hold the mirror
Circular mirror, 24 cm diameter, to sit inside the ring
Strong clear adhesive or jewellery wire for securing the mirror to the ring

content.text: Step 1: Secure the mirror to the ring

Place the circular mirror inside the metal ring. Secure it with small spots of strong clear adhesive at four evenly spaced points around the edge, or bind it to the ring at four points with fine jewellery wire. Allow the adhesive to cure fully before adding the cords. The mirror must sit flush against the ring and not shift when the frame is hanging.

Cut 20 lengths of cord each 160 cm long. Fold each at its midpoint and mount with a lark's head knot around the top half of the ring (the 180 degrees of arc from roughly 9 o'clock to 3 o'clock, working across the top). Pack the knots tightly together so no ring is visible between them. The remaining bottom 180 degrees of the ring is left uncovered at this stage; the fringe cords will hang from the lower section.

Take the outermost cords at each side and work a row of 6 to 8 half-hitches over the ring at the 9 o'clock and 3 o'clock positions, pulling the cords snugly against the ring. This locks the side cords against the ring and prevents the mounted section from sliding. All remaining cords hang free below the centre of the ring as the fringe.
Step 4: Finish the fringe and add a hanging cord

Trim the fringe to the desired length below the ring: an even cut at 15 to 20 cm below the bottom of the ring produces a neat fringe. For a brushed feathered look, unravel 3 to 4 cm of each cord end and use a stiff brush to separate the fibres. Tie a hanging loop of cord to the top of the ring using an overhand knot, leaving enough length to hang freely from a hook or nail.

items.problem: The lark's head knots slide around the ring rather than staying in position
items.cause: The ring is smooth and the cord is slipping, or the knots are too loosely packed
items.fix: Pack the knots tightly together and push each one up against the previous one before moving to the next. If the ring surface is very smooth, a light application of clear craft adhesive under the knot row holds them in position once dry.

items.problem: The fringe hangs unevenly with some cords much longer than others
items.cause: The cords were not cut to equal lengths before mounting, or the lark's head knots were not placed at the exact midpoint of each cord
items.fix: Always fold each cord and check that both tails are equal length before mounting. Cut a cardboard template to the cord length and wrap each cord around it before cutting to ensure uniform lengths.
